notes: Founded 1983 in a Massachusetts garage by Craig Benson and Robert Levine; peak revenue $1B+ (1996); employed 6,600 at peak; reorganized 2000 as holding company with four subsidiaries; Enterasys Networks (core business) went public 2001; acquired by The Gores Group ($386M, 2006); acquired by Extreme Networks ($180M, 2013).

Cabletron's core enterprise networking business spun out 2001; took original Cabletron products, SmartSwitch technology, Spectrum management; traded on NYSE; taken private by The Gores Group (2006); merged with Siemens Enterprise Communications assets; acquired by Extreme Networks ($180M, 2013).

Cabletron subsidiary from YAGO Systems acquisition; focused on Switched Ethernet; acquired by Lucent Technologies; became redundant after Alcatel-Lucent merger; placed into Chapter 11 bankruptcy and liquidated.
Cabletron subsidiary holding Spectrum network management software assets; acquired by Concord Communications; Concord acquired by Computer Associates (now CA) for $350M.
Primary competitive reference in report; Aberdeen notes Cisco/StrataCom merger as example of PPSN market trend; Cisco dominated the networking market and PPSN opportunity that Cabletron sought.

Spun out of AT&T Bell Labs in 1996; Aberdeen cites Lucent/Agile Networks merger as example of PPSN trend; Lucent merged with Alcatel (2006) to form Alcatel-Lucent; Nokia acquired Alcatel-Lucent in 2016.
Cited as Cabletron's ADSL development partner; semiconductor division spun off as Freescale Semiconductor (2004); Freescale acquired by NXP (2015).
Remote access networking company; acquired by Cabletron; technology integrated into CyberSWITCH product line.
Networking hardware company; acquired by Cabletron; used Intel i960 processor (matching Cabletron's ASIC philosophy); technology rapidly assimilated into Cabletron product line.
ATM networking company; acquired by Cabletron; used Intel i960 and C++ development consistent with Cabletron's acquisition criteria.
